content.text: Azure Dev/Test pricing gives development and test teams access to significantly reduced rates on many Azure services — sometimes 50-55% below standard pay-as-you-go — in exchange for agreeing that the subscription will not run production workloads and that most services carry no SLA.

Dev/Test pricing is available through three offer types: Enterprise Dev/Test subscriptions (for EA customers), Visual Studio subscription monthly credits, and Pay-As-You-Go Dev/Test (for individual Visual Studio subscribers). Each requires that users accessing the resources have an active Visual Studio subscription.

attrs.alt: Azure Dev/Test pricing subscription types and discount overview

content.text: Not all Azure services have a separate dev/test rate — discounts apply to specific services where the cost reduction is most meaningful for development teams.
Pay compute cost only; same as AHB

The absence of an SLA is the key constraint. If your dev/test environment runs integration tests that must complete within a tight maintenance window, an unplanned outage has no compensation — plan accordingly and do not use these subscriptions for revenue-critical workloads.
Setting Up an Enterprise Dev/Test Subscription

If your organisation has an Enterprise Agreement, an EA administrator can create Dev/Test subscriptions from the EA portal. Each subscription is linked to a specific team or project and is subject to the standard EA spending controls.

# List existing subscriptions and their offer types
az account list \
  --query "[].{Name:name, ID:id, State:state, OfferType:tenantId}" \
  --output table

# Check if current subscription is a dev/test subscription
az account show \
  --query "{Name:name, SubscriptionId:id, State:state}" \
  --output json

# Create a resource group scoped to the dev/test subscription
az group create \
  --name rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --location eastus \
  --tags environment=dev team=alpha

# Tag all resources to identify dev/test scope for billing
az tag create \
  --resource-id /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --tags environment=dev cost-center=engineering
Creating Windows VMs with Dev/Test Pricing

On a Dev/Test subscription, Windows VMs automatically get the OS license waiver — you do not need to set --license-type Windows_Server explicitly. The billing system detects the subscription type and applies the dev/test rate.

# Create a Windows VM on a Dev/Test subscription
# The OS license cost is automatically waived
az vm create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name dev-win-server \
  --image Win2022Datacenter \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --admin-username devadmin \
  --admin-password "<password>" \
  --tags environment=dev purpose=integration-testing

# Verify the effective rate in the Azure Pricing Calculator
# or check Cost Management for the actual billed amount

# Shut down the VM when not in use (key dev/test cost practice)
az vm deallocate \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name dev-win-server

# Start it again for testing
az vm start \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name dev-win-server

Azure DevTest Labs is a managed service for dev/test VM environments. It lets you define reusable VM templates (called formulas), enforce auto-shutdown schedules, set per-user and per-lab VM quotas, and provide self-service VM provisioning to developers — all on Dev/Test pricing.

# Create a DevTest Lab
az lab create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name lab-team-alpha \
  --location eastus

# Set auto-shutdown policy for all VMs in the lab (daily at 7pm)
az lab policy set \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --lab-name lab-team-alpha \
  --name LabVmsShutdown \
  --policy-type Schedule \
  --status Enabled \
  --fact-data '{"taskType":"LabVmsShutdownTask","timeZoneId":"UTC","dailyRecurrence":{"time":"1900"}}'

# Set a per-user VM count limit
az lab policy set \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --lab-name lab-team-alpha \
  --name MaxVmsAllowedPerUser \
  --policy-type MaxVmsAllowedPerUser \
  --status Enabled \
  --threshold 3

# Create a VM formula (reusable template) in the lab
az lab formula create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --lab-name lab-team-alpha \
  --name win2022-dev-template \
  --os-type Windows \
  --notes "Standard dev VM for .NET development" \
  --lab-virtual-machine-creation-parameter \
    '{
      "size": "Standard_D4s_v5",
      "userName": "devadmin",
      "password": "<password>",
      "labSubnetName": "default",
      "labVirtualNetworkId": "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.DevTestLab/labs/<lab>/virtualnetworks/<vnet>",
      "galleryImageReference": {
        "offer": "WindowsServer",
        "publisher": "MicrosoftWindowsServer",
        "sku": "2022-Datacenter",
        "osType": "Windows",
        "version": "latest"
      }
    }'

# List available VMs in the lab
az lab vm list \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --lab-name lab-team-alpha \
  --output table
The single biggest cost driver in dev/test environments is VMs left running overnight or over weekends. Auto-shutdown policies eliminate this waste automatically.

# Enable auto-shutdown on an individual VM (outside DevTest Labs)
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name dev-win-server \
  --time 1900 \
  --email "developer@example.com"

# Disable auto-shutdown
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name dev-win-server \
  --off

# Apply auto-shutdown to ALL VMs in a resource group
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.devtestlabs import DevTestLabsClient
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
import json, requests

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

subscription_id = "<subscription-id>"
resource_group = "rg-devtest-team-alpha"
shutdown_time = "1900"  # 7 PM UTC
notification_email = "team@example.com"

compute_client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

for vm in compute_client.virtual_machines.list(resource_group):
    vm_resource_id = vm.id
    schedule_name = "shutdown-computevm-" + vm.name

    schedule_body = {
        "properties": {
            "status": "Enabled",
            "taskType": "ComputeVmShutdownTask",
            "dailyRecurrence": {"time": shutdown_time},
            "timeZoneId": "UTC",
            "notificationSettings": {
                "status": "Enabled",
                "timeInMinutes": 30,
                "emailRecipient": notification_email,
            },
            "targetResourceId": vm_resource_id,
        },
        "location": vm.location,
    }

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/resourceGroups/{resource_group}/providers/microsoft.devtestlab"
        f"/schedules/{schedule_name}?api-version=2018-09-15"
    )
    resp = requests.put(url, json=schedule_body, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"})
    if resp.status_code in (200, 201):
        print(f"Auto-shutdown set for {vm.name} at {shutdown_time} UTC")
    else:
        print(f"Failed for {vm.name}: {resp.status_code} {resp.text[:100]}")
Budget Limits and Spending Caps for Dev/Test

Individual Visual Studio subscriptions include a spending limit that prevents billing beyond the monthly credit. For team Dev/Test subscriptions, you should set explicit budgets to catch runaway costs from forgotten resources.

# Create a monthly budget with alert at 80% and 100%
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name devtest-monthly-budget \
  --amount 500 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-07-01 \
  --end-date 2025-06-30 \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --notifications \
    enabled=true operator=GreaterThan threshold=80 contactEmails=["team-lead@example.com"] thresholdType=Actual

# List all resources running in the dev/test RG with their estimated cost
az costmanagement query \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --type Usage \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping name=ResourceId type=Dimension \
  --output table
Dev/Test Pricing with Azure SQL Database

Azure SQL Database on a Dev/Test subscription bills at a significantly reduced rate compared to the production licence-included rate. This makes it practical to run separate dev, staging, and QA SQL instances without the cost exploding.

# Create an Azure SQL Server and database on a Dev/Test subscription
az sql server create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name sql-devtest-server \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "<password>"

# Create a General Purpose database (dev/test rate applies automatically on Dev/Test sub)
az sql db create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --server sql-devtest-server \
  --name app-db-dev \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 4 \
  --backup-storage-redundancy Local

# Pause the database when not in use (Serverless tier - auto-pause)
az sql db update \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --server sql-devtest-server \
  --name app-db-dev \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --compute-model Serverless \
  --auto-pause-delay 60

SQL Database Serverless tier with auto-pause is ideal for dev/test: the database pauses after 60 minutes of inactivity and resumes automatically on first connection. You pay only for storage while paused.

# Check current database pricing tier and state
az sql db show \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --server sql-devtest-server \
  --name app-db-dev \
  --query "{Name:name, Edition:edition, ServiceObjective:currentServiceObjectiveName, Status:status}" \
  --output table

# Scale down to Burstable (lower cost) for long-running test cycles
az sql db update \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --server sql-devtest-server \
  --name app-db-dev \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 2
Tagging Strategy for Dev/Test Cost Attribution

A consistent tagging strategy lets you separate dev/test costs from production in Cost Management reports and chargebacks. Apply tags at resource creation time via CLI, ARM templates, or Azure Policy.

# Tag policy: enforce environment tag on all resources in the dev/test RG
az policy assignment create \
  --name "require-environment-tag" \
  --display-name "Require environment tag on all resources" \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "environment"}}'

# Apply tags to all untagged resources in the RG
az resource list \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --query "[?tags.environment == null].id" \
  --output tsv | while read resource_id; do
    az resource tag \
      --ids "$resource_id" \
      --tags environment=dev cost-center=engineering team=alpha
done
Ephemeral Dev Environments with Azure Container Instances

For short-lived test runs (unit tests, integration tests, one-off scripts), Azure Container Instances (ACI) is often cheaper than keeping a VM running. ACI is billed per second and terminated automatically when the container exits.

# Run a containerized test suite in ACI (billed only while running)
az container create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name test-runner-$(date +%s) \
  --image mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 \
  --cpu 2 \
  --memory 4 \
  --command-line "dotnet test /app/MyProject.Tests/MyProject.Tests.csproj --logger trx" \
  --restart-policy Never \
  --environment-variables \
    ENVIRONMENT=dev \
    CONNECTION_STRING="<dev-db-connection-string>" \
  --output json

# Wait for completion and retrieve logs
az container logs \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name test-runner-<timestamp>

# Container is billed only while running — delete after test
az container delete \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name test-runner-<timestamp> \
  --yes

# Cost comparison: ACI vs D4s_v5 VM for a 2-hour test run
# ACI (2 vCPU, 4 GB): $0.0000125 per vCPU-sec + $0.0000013 per GB-sec
# 2 hours = 7200 seconds
# ACI cost: (2 * 7200 * 0.0000125) + (4 * 7200 * 0.0000013) = $0.18 + $0.037 = ~$0.22

# D4s_v5 Windows VM dev/test rate: ~$0.08/hr (compute only, OS waived)
# 2 hours: $0.16

# ACI slightly more expensive here, but no idle cost between runs.
# For bursty test suites with gaps, ACI saves significantly over always-on VMs.
echo "Use ACI for short, bursty test runs; dev/test VMs for long-running interactive dev work"
Visual Studio subscriptions (purchased through MSDN or Visual Studio Marketplace) include monthly Azure credits that reset each month. Credits cannot be carried over; unused credit expires.
Larger dev VMs, SQL databases, AKS testing

# Check remaining credit on a Visual Studio subscription
# (Portal: Cost Management -> Overview shows credit balance)
# CLI: no direct credit balance command; use the Portal

# Set a spending limit notification to avoid credit exhaustion mid-month
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name vs-monthly-credit-guard \
  --amount 140 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-07-01 \
  --end-date 2025-06-30 \
  --notifications \
    enabled=true operator=GreaterThan threshold=90 contactEmails=["dev@example.com"] thresholdType=Actual

# When credit is exhausted, spending halts automatically if spending limit is ON
# Remove the spending limit only if you want to pay beyond the credit

Visual Studio subscription credits are per-subscriber, not transferable. If a developer exhausts their $150/month Enterprise credit before month end, new resource creation is blocked until the next billing cycle — plan workloads accordingly and use auto-shutdown aggressively.
Auto-shutdown at 7 PM; budget alert at 80%
Use Standard_LRS or Standard_SSD for dev VMs
Tag enforcement + Policy to restrict resource types
Running SQL always-on for infrequent tests
Use --restart-policy Never and delete after test
DevTest Labs per-user quota; DevBox for standardised dev
Enforcing Dev/Test Guardrails with Azure Policy
Policy can prevent developers from accidentally deploying expensive SKUs or production-grade services into the dev/test subscription, acting as a cost guardrail at the platform level.

# Deploy the allowed-SKUs policy
az policy definition create \
  --name "allowed-vm-skus-devtest" \
  --display-name "Allowed VM SKUs for Dev/Test" \
  --rules allowed-skus-policy.json \
  --mode All

az policy assignment create \
  --name "enforce-vm-sku-devtest" \
  --policy "allowed-vm-skus-devtest" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --display-name "Dev/Test: allowed VM SKUs only"

# Verify: try creating a blocked SKU (should fail)
az vm create \
  --resource-group rg-devtest-team-alpha \
  --name test-blocked \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_M128s \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys
# Expected: RequestDisallowedByPolicy error

Combine allowed-SKU policy with a budget alert and auto-shutdown schedule for a three-layer cost control mechanism: the Policy prevents expensive VMs from being created, auto-shutdown stops forgotten VMs at end of day, and the budget alert warns before the spending cap is reached.

Azure Dev/Test pricing provides deep discounts (up to 55%) on Windows VMs, SQL Database, App Service, and Logic Apps for non-production workloads accessed by Visual Studio subscribers. Use Enterprise Dev/Test subscriptions for EA teams, Visual Studio monthly credits for individual developers, and PAYG Dev/Test for flexible team sandboxes. Combine Dev/Test pricing with auto-shutdown schedules and budget alerts to keep development costs well controlled, and never run production workloads or SLA-critical services under a Dev/Test offer.
